title: Welcome to Dart Quiz 🏆
description: Test your knowledge of Dart entry points, printing, variables, and data types!

questions.question: What is the name of the function where every Dart program begins?
questions.explanation: Every Dart program must start execution from the main() function.

questions.question: Which framework heavily uses Dart to build mobile apps?
questions.explanation: Dart is the programming language that powers Google's Flutter framework.

questions.question: Fill in the blank to print text to the screen: _____('Hello!');
questions.explanation: The print() function sends output to the console.

questions.question: Every line of code in Dart MUST end with a semicolon (;).
questions.explanation: Semicolons are required in Dart to indicate the end of a statement.

questions.question: Which keyword lets Dart automatically figure out the type of a variable?
questions.explanation: The 'var' keyword infers the variable type based on the value assigned to it.

questions.question: Which of these is a VALID variable name in Dart?
questions.explanation: Variable names cannot contain spaces, hyphens, or start with numbers. CamelCase is the standard.

questions.question: You can reassign a variable created with 'var' to a new value later in the code.
questions.explanation: Variables are mutable by default, meaning their contents can be changed after they are created.

questions.question: What is the correct way to explicitly declare a text variable?
questions.explanation: The String type in Dart must be capitalized.

questions.question: Which of these pairs represents the two number types in Dart?
questions.explanation: Dart uses 'int' for whole numbers and 'double' for floating-point decimals.

questions.question: What type of data should you use for a weight that has decimal points? (e.g. 70.5)
questions.explanation: The double type is used to store floating-point numbers (numbers with decimals).

questions.question: What values can a 'bool' variable hold?
questions.explanation: A bool (boolean) variable can only strictly be 'true' or 'false'.

questions.question: If you try to assign the String 'hello' to a variable declared as an 'int', Dart will crash or throw an error.
questions.explanation: Dart is strongly typed. An int variable can never hold a String.

questions.question: How do you start a single-line comment in Dart?
questions.explanation: Double slashes (//) signify the start of a single-line comment.

questions.question: Fill in the blank to end a multi-line comment: /* Note text ___
questions.explanation: The /* begins a multi-line comment, and */ ends it.

questions.question: What happens to comments when you run a Dart program?
options: They are printed to the console.
They are completely ignored by the computer.
questions.explanation: Comments are for developers. The compiler entirely ignores them when executing.

questions.question: You can define multiple variables with the same name in the exact same scope.
questions.explanation: Variables must have unique names. If you declare two boxes with the same label, Dart gets confused.

questions.question: Which snippet is completely valid Dart code?
questions.explanation: 'int' cannot hold decimals, 'bool' shouldn't have quotes for true, and 'var num = ;' is a syntax error.

questions.question: What is the output of: print('1' + '1');
questions.explanation: Because they are Strings (wrapped in quotes), the '+' operator concatenates (joins) them together.

questions.question: A double can sometimes hold a whole number (e.g. double score = 10;).
questions.explanation: While 'double' is meant for decimals, Dart automatically converts an integer like 10 into 10.0 if stored in a double.

questions.question: Is 'dart' case sensitive?
options: No, 'Name' and 'name' are the same variable.
questions.explanation: Dart is case-sensitive, meaning capital letters matter when naming variables or using keywords.
